questions.question: A bag contains 5 red marbles, 3 blue marbles, and 2 green marbles. You draw two marbles without replacement. What is the probability that both marbles are red?
questions.explanation: Total marbles = 10. P(both red) = (5/10) × (4/9) = 20/90 = 2/9. The denominator is 10 × 9 because you draw without replacement.

questions.question: A two-way table shows that of 200 students surveyed, 120 play sports and 80 do not. Of those who play sports, 75 have a GPA above 3.5. Of those who don't play sports, 40 have a GPA above 3.5. If a student is selected at random and has a GPA above 3.5, what is the probability they play sports? Round to the nearest hundredth.
questions.explanation: Total with GPA > 3.5: 75 + 40 = 115. P(plays sports | GPA > 3.5) = 75/115 ≈ 0.652, which rounds to 0.65.

questions.question: Event A has probability 0.4 and Event B has probability 0.6. If A and B are independent, what is the probability that exactly one of them occurs?

A factory produces widgets where 2% are defective. A quality test correctly identifies 98% of defective widgets and correctly identifies 99% of non-defective widgets as good. If a widget fails the test, what is the probability it is actually defective?
questions.explanation: Use Bayes' theorem. P(defective|fails) = P(fails|defective) × P(defective) / P(fails). P(fails|defective) = 0.98, P(defective) = 0.02, P(fails) = (0.98)(0.02) + (0.01)(0.98) ≈ 0.0296. Result: (0.98 × 0.02) / 0.0296 ≈ 0.66 or 0.67.

questions.question: Three cards are drawn without replacement from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability that all three are face cards (jack, queen, or king)?
In a group of 50 students, 30 study math, 25 study science, and 15 study both. If you randomly select a student who studies math, what is the probability they also study science?
questions.explanation: P(science | math) = P(both) / P(math) = 15/30 = 1/2. There are 15 students studying both and 30 studying math total.
